data.title: TRX Gold: Self-Funded Growth Beyond The Gold Rally
data.description:
TRX Gold Corporation (TRX) is a high-margin, early-scale gold producer where the equity story does not rely only on gold prices moving favorably from here but on scaling its already strong unit economics into higher production. The past year has already seen both the tailwinds play out favorably for TRX. Investors who got in a year ago may have already seen ~3x returns (~4x at some point in early 2026). So the question for a buy thesis today is how much of the upside remains to be reaped and what risks?

I have a supportive thesis on gold prices. I see the geopolitical uncertainties as continuing to remain elevated, irrespective of how and when the Iran war ends. Inflation is also proving to be sticky (more so after the Iran war and oil shocks). Importantly, gold prices have shown a history of moving in bursts (last seen around the 2008 recession) and undergoing time corrections more than deep pullbacks after sharp rallies. The current rally has seen gold prices touch ~$5.5k/oz and have now corrected by ~15% toward the ~$4.5k/oz mark. I strongly believe that those levels can be considered a floor (even after the rally around 2008-2012, the maximum drawdown we saw was ~25%, and far lower instances and depth of negative 1-year rolling returns).

Gold's rise in Q4 last year was also in part due to uncertainty around equity valuations, overall growth outlook, and the AI trade (capex drag, valuation uncertainties, cooling fears, AI return economics uncertainties, and AI commoditization risks). So, I think it is reasonable to assume a supported regime for gold.

My thesis works on the ~$4.5k/oz mark as a floor, and any further upside is an optionality for conservatism. What tilts TRX to a buy is that even assuming no further gold price tailwinds, the production growth trajectory is sufficient to justify further upside for the stock from here. The current rally may have partially factored that in, but there is still room for further gains to be reaped. I rely on the execution track record and the self-funded nature of growth to believe that the production ramp should be on track and that should help TRX deliver stellar returns even after the huge rally we have seen recently.

Valuations and What Is Embedded

To understand embedded expectations and true valuations today, we have to look beyond reported TTM and forward EV to EBITDA numbers. The gold price surge in Q4 has meant the TTM metrics lag the current realities by a huge margin. Because there is a production ramp at play, the forward valuations assume a certain degree of volume increase at a certain cost and capital intensity (which is where an opportunistic edge may lie).

So, let us outline what valuation number is actually a true reflection of what is happening and argue from there. The current EV is ~$500m, and the Q1 FY2026 EBITDA (~$13m) run rate is ~$50m annualized. That means the implied valuation based on those numbers is closer to ~10x EV to EBITDA. This too may appear stretched for even established, growth-oriented producers. So, we are not looking at cheap multiples - the valuations already reflect some amount of execution toward the production ramp and also assume gold prices remain supported if not rally from here on. While the latter looks reasonable (with my gold thesis), the former must be gauged in detail to ensure whether future economics can sustain an upside in TRX shares (assuming multiple rerating had limited headroom from ~10x EV to EBITDA zones).

However, the baseline I have used for valuations may also be conservative, given the sequential production and realized prices improvements as per preliminary Q2 FY2026 data. Production is reported to be up by ~13% sequentially, and realized prices are up ~21% QoQ. While full financials are awaited (mid-April), the run rate should be higher than Q1 and the EBITDA should also be far higher than the assumed baseline of ~$50m annualized.

What Supports Current Earnings

The first thing that needs to be understood when talking about further gains is how durable the current EBITDA levels are. That is almost a direct function of how well supported gold prices are. I have highlighted my views around $4.5k/oz as a defendable floor and take any upside as an optionality. At those gold price levels and a cost base of ~1.5k/oz, we have a per ounce margin of $3k. At production levels of 25-30k oz, that translates to a gross margin of ~$75m-$90m. That seems to align well with an expectation of ~$50m EBITDA durability, even accounting for overheads and other adjustments.

The cost base expectations are assumed to be ~$1.5k because, as per management indications in the Q1 FY2026 earnings call -

We do continue to access higher-grade ore blocks and remain on track for that production guidance of again, between 25,000 ounces and 30,000 ounces at a cash cost of between $1,400 and $1,600 an ounce.

There is a possible implication of higher costs as inflation lifts costs further, but I feel that is offset in my assumptions of ~$4.5k/oz gold prices. Gold is currently trading well above that mark, and average prices for Q2 FY2026 at least will be far higher. Overall, steady-state cost inflation and potential gold price increases could offset each other, and even in the scenario of a slightly lower spread, the EBITDA range of ~$50m can be assumed as a durable baseline from Q3 onward (looking at the gross margins available). The Q2 production and pricing step-up provides early evidence for this durability. So, we are talking about a gold upside scenario, and should be okay with minor corrections as well.

How the Upside Works Out

The TRX story stands out from that of a smaller miner's because of phased scaling plans for the Buckreef asset, where incremental plant expansion is in progress - importantly funded by internal cash flows (not dilution). TRX has mentioned plans to double production over time too. The current phase aims to upgrade production from the current levels of 2000 tons per day to 3000. Expansion decisions are already underway (plant upgrades and equipment evaluation). Given execution so far has seen production scale from 360 tons per day to 2000 now, we are essentially talking about a continuation rather than unknown execution risks here. Management has also outlined 12-18 months as the timeline for this execution phase toward 3000 tons per day. Recent updates (preliminary Q2 results and updates) also indicate that key components for the 3000+ target are already delivered or nearing commissioning across Q3-Q4 FY2026, reinforcing that the ramp is actively underway.

Even if we account for execution issues and delays, we are talking about a 2-3 year ramp period where production could go up by 50%. Assuming costs scale proportionately without fixed cost efficiencies kicking in, we are talking about EBITDA moving toward the ~$80-100m range in a conservative case (with upside to that if operational leverage or grade improvements come through). Eventually, further expansion is not ruled out as per management's own ambitions of doubling capacity. However, even in a more conservative and realistic case, the doubling of EBITDA could absorb some multiple compression from ~10x EV to EBITDA to say ~8x and still generate 60% share returns.

This, of course, needs the valuation to hold up at closer to current valuations and not grow into EBITDA increases. For instance, a valuation of ~5x after production ramp means the share price upside is absorbed. In that sense, the key variable is not just execution, but whether the market treats the resulting EBITDA as structural and repeatable rather than cyclical.

I believe the case for a derating toward ~5x multiples looks limited because the EBITDA expansion is not gold price driven (only assumes continuity of the ~$4.5k/oz base). Operational growth should be seen as more durable than a multiple hinging on gold prices alone. Another reason to believe valuations will not shrink to mid-single-digit cyclical averages is that expansion is being funded through internal cash flows. This reduces one of the biggest risks in small-cap mining stories - equity dilution. The company's inclination toward bootstrapped expansion (through the 360-2000 tons per day journey) means incremental EBITDA accrues more directly to existing shareholders. Capex requirements ahead also should be incremental (plant expansion versus greenfield projects). Preliminary Q2 updates show all outstanding warrants are now exercised (or expired). That means ~$21m of cash added and no further warrant overhang at the moment. The working capital is also looking strong at ~2.5x current ratio.

Buy Call and Risks

TRX seems to be a straightforward buy call on structural volume expansion with reasonable upside to be reaped even if gold prices don't rally further. The risks to the thesis start with gold prices falling sharply (say, well below $4k/oz), which I have considered unlikely at the start of this thesis. The chain of lower EBITDA and valuations and delayed expansion due to funding availability kicks in at those levels. Execution risks are there but limited because we have already seen the scaling execution this far, so we are neither talking about greenfield projects nor an untested model. Multiple compression risks are derived from all the above risks, which simplifies the monitoring model for fresh investments today.

Investors should track gold prices correcting sharply below the $4k/oz mark and sustaining there for a couple of months (or a sharper correction). They should track subsequent earnings calls to ascertain whether any outsized delays or execution delays are cropping up. While cost inflation could impact margins, as long as the upper end of the guided $1.6k/oz band is not challenged (and gold prices do not cover the cost increase), the buy thesis should stay intact. Near-term validation will come from the detailed Q2 financial release, particularly whether higher production and realized price translate proportionately into EBITDA and cash flow. Preliminary data already points to a step change in revenue and operating leverage (realized prices are up significantly). I fully expect stronger EBITDA conversion confirming earnings momentum. Cost indications should help support my assumptions on a strong margin persisting even accounting for the recent corrections in gold prices.

data.title: Eagle Point Credit: Insiders Are Purchasing While NAV Is In Free Fall
data.description:
Thesis

Catalyst: The CFO/COO and portfolio manager make significant share purchases days after the February net asset value is disclosed.

Eagle Point Credit Company Inc. (ECC) stands to benefit from the recent reversal in leveraged loan spreads. Spreads compressed to post-GFC lows in the fourth quarter of 2025 which is an expression of confidence by investors in the asset class and a credit tailwind for leveraged loan issuers, as it reduces the overall interest expense burden for borrowers. Nevertheless, shares of ECC are trading close to their 52-week lows, currently at a 20% discount to the February 2026 NAV estimate, and have not seen any significant recovery year to date. The current net asset value of $4.36 reflects the latest mark-to-market valuation of the portfolio, which tends to decline in periods of volatility given the lack of liquidity of the underlying assets. The NAV trajectory has been extremely negative over the last 3 months, with NAV declining 23.5% from $5.70 in December 2025 to $4.36 in only 2 months. The leveraged loan market dispersion in February created significant mark-to-market valuation pressure, which may represent the worst of the dislocation.

The core of the investment thesis is based on two factors: first, the recent open-market purchases by CFO/COO Ken Onorio (110,000 shares at $3.74, ~$411K) and Portfolio Manager Dan Ko (57,165 shares at $3.50, ~$200K) signal that insiders believe the panic-driven selloff has overshot fundamentals. Onorio purchased at a 14.2% discount to the February NAV midpoint. Ko purchased at a 19.7% discount. Second, the private credit crisis driving negative sentiment toward ECC is paradoxically creating a tailwind for the company’s underlying CLO portfolios through par build opportunities as private credit funds liquidate their most liquid BSL holdings at discounted prices.

For investors with a 12-month horizon and tolerance for mark-to-market volatility, the risk/reward at current levels is skewed to the upside.

Company Overview

Eagle Point Credit Company Inc. is an externally managed closed-end fund registered under the 1940 Act, advised by Eagle Point Credit Management LLC out of Greenwich, CT. The fund invests primarily in equity and junior debt tranches of CLOs collateralized by portfolios of below-investment-grade US senior secured loans. The adviser manages ~$14 billion across the Eagle Point platform and employs what it describes as a private-equity-style approach to CLO investing—proactively sourcing opportunities, taking control stakes, and influencing key terms at the CLO formation stage.

Since its IPO on October 7, 2014, at $20.00 per share, ECC has paid $23.73 in cumulative common distributions per share through January 31, 2026—more than the original IPO price. The stock has historically traded at an average premium to NAV of 9.86% since IPO. The portfolio is diversified across ~197 CLO investments with indirect exposure to 1,850 unique underlying loan obligors. No single obligor accounts for more than 0.58% of exposure.

Debt Market Turmoil

The private credit panic is negatively affecting sentiment even though ECC does not hold private credit loans. Every loan underlying the CLOs is a cash interest-paying broadly syndicated leveraged loan (BSL) which is large, liquid, tradable and originated by major banks and tracked by indices like the S&P/LSTA leveraged loan index. The CLO structures holding these loans have no mark-to-market triggers or margin calls that many BDCs and private credit vehicles are exposed to.

The Morningstar LSTA US Leveraged Loan Index dropped to 94.17 on March 3, the lowest since June 2023, and below its April 2025 tariff levels. In September, when the buyout of EA was announced, the largest of its kind, the index was trading over 97 cents on the dollar. Single-B leveraged loan spreads in early March have widened to ~450bps. The total NAV decline from Q4 2024 ($8.38) to February 2026 ($4.36) is 48% and reflects the severe mark-to-market environment for CLO equity, which has been driven by leveraged loan spread compression and the private credit contagion in late 2025 and early 2026.

The NAV development has been negative across the universe of listed CLO-focused closed-end funds. The trend appears to have accelerated since Tricolor and First Brands bankruptcies last summer, which triggered a risk-off sentiment toward CLO assets, which further accelerated as worries about the potential disruption of AI to highly leveraged software companies continue to disrupt markets.

Market-wide CLO equity total returns were approximately -15% for 2025, confirming that valuation pressure across the sector was broad-based rather than idiosyncratic.

On balance, the private credit crisis is a potential tailwind for CLO managers: private credit funds are facing significant redemption requests from investors, which is forcing them to liquidate their BSL holdings first (most liquid). Deutsche Bank AG analysts estimate that BDCs own nearly $143 billion of leveraged loans, which could be sold to meet redemptions and push spreads wider. iCapital estimates that redemptions will remain elevated for a three- to five-quarter period—enough for CLO managers to increase residual cash flows to CLO equity. On balance, the private credit crisis is a potential tailwind for CLO managers: private credit funds are facing significant redemption requests from investors, which is forcing them to liquidate their BSL holdings first (most liquid). "Business development companies own nearly $143 billion of leveraged loans, which could be sold to meet redemption requests and push spreads wider, according to Deutsche Bank AG analysts." iCapital estimates that redemption will remain elevated for a three- to five-quarter period - enough for CLO managers to increase residual cash flows to CLO equity.

Higher quality loans (BB-, B+) are becoming available at discounted prices, which allows CLO managers to acquire them to “build par”: purchasing loans below par are counted at full par for OC test calculations, which improves OC cushions and strengthens CLO equity cash flows. The supply/demand balance is further being shifted in favor of permanent capital vehicles like ECC with significant remaining reinvestment periods, as annual CLO issuance has declined by 30% amid increased volatility in credit markets.

Leveraged loan credit fundamentals have largely remained stable, with total leverage remaining around ~5x during recent quarters and interest coverage improving to about 3x, as can be seen in the charts below:

Shifting Focus Beyond CLO Equity

On the back of increasingly challenging conditions in the CLO equity space in 2025, with loan spreads grinding tighter from 3.45% to 3.16% and the CLO equity arbitrage collapsing as the excess spread available to CLO equity holders was compressing quarter by quarter, management decided to reduce ECC's CLO equity allocation from 79.5% in February 2025 to 65.0% by January 2026. Regulatory capital relief transactions grew from 2.9% to 9.3%, becoming the largest non-CLO sleeve. These are synthetic risk transfer (SRT) deals where banks pay ECC to absorb first-loss exposure on loan portfolios—structurally similar to CLO equity but sourced through bilateral negotiation rather than public markets, typically at wider spreads and with less mark-to-market volatility. Infrastructure credit (0% to 4.4%) and portfolio debt securities (0% to 9.0%) were added as entirely new categories in late 2025, reflecting Eagle Point's broader platform capabilities. Rather than deploying capital into new CLO equity at historically tight spreads, management redirected it toward credit asset classes where the risk-adjusted returns were more attractive. The annual report noted that non-CLO investments generated an 18% gross IRR — meaningfully above the effective yields on new CLO equity during the same period.

In addition, during 2025 and early 2026, ECC advanced strategic initiatives, including continued support of Muzinich’s US CLO collateral management platform (over $40M fully deployed) and the launch of Muzinich’s European CLO collateral management platform, which are expected to generate fee income for ECC by way of revenue sharing. Management frames this as enhancing yield potential and diversification by leveraging the adviser’s broader credit expertise. The adviser itself is positioned as a firm focused on specialized credit strategies in inefficient markets—portfolio debt securities, regulatory capital relief, strategic credit, and CLO securities.

ECC’s distribution has been reduced from $0.14/share/month to $0.06/share/month beginning April 2026 ($0.72 annualized). The distribution still yields 20.2% at the current share price, but this more than 50% reduction in distribution has weighed heavily on investor sentiment and contributed to the widening discount to NAV. While painful, these actions actually align with long-term investors interests, as the reduced distribution allows management to reinvest capital and stabilize the balance sheet.

Recent Insider Transactions

The insider purchases are the catalyst at the core of this analysis. On March 13, 2026, CFO/COO Ken Onorio purchased 110,000 shares at $3.74, investing ~$411K at a 14.2% discount to the February NAV midpoint of $4.36. Five days later, on March 18, Dan Ko purchased 57,165 shares at $3.50, investing ~$200K at a 19.7% discount to NAV. Combined ~$611K in personal capital committed at prices well below the most recent estimated NAV.

The timing is a positive signal itself, as both purchases came days after the February NAV estimate was disclosed. Both officers had full visibility into the fund’s most current mark-to-market valuations, OC cushion levels, and cash flow projections before committing personal capital. This is not a case of insiders buying on stale or optimistic data—it signals that the people with the deepest insight into ECC’s book believe the shares are mispriced relative to intrinsic value and that the current panic is likely misapplied.

Q4 2025 Results Review

For the year ended December 31, 2025, ECC recorded a decrease in net assets from operations of $134 million, or $1.08 per weighted average common share, primarily driven by unrealized losses on investments from ongoing spread compression in the underlying loan market. GAAP return on common equity of -14.6% was broadly in line with and modestly better than sector estimates of CLO equity performance despite the company’s use of leverage.

Underlying credit fundamentals remained healthy. Market-wide CCC-rated loan exposure averaged 4.3% vs. 4.1% for ECC. Average junior OC cushions: 3.9% market-wide vs. 4.5% for ECC. Only 3.6% of loans underlying ECC’s CLOs were trading below 80 at year-end vs. ~4.4% for the broader market. Conservative positioning relative to market averages across the board.

Notably, recurring cash flows actually improved in Q4 2025—from $0.59/share in Q3 to $0.61/share—despite spread compression and temporary cash flow diversions from reset and refinance transactions. CLO market activity remained strong in 2025 with new issuance totaling a record $209 billion. ECC directed a record number of refinancings and resets that lowered its weighted average CLO AAA spread to 131 bps (vs. 140 bps at prior year-end), though these cost savings only partially offset the income decline from underlying loan spread compression.

Capital structure moves: On January 30, 2026, ECC redeemed its 8.00% Series F Term Preferred Stock at $25.00 per share, reducing financing costs. On February 17, 2026, the company announced a $100 million common stock repurchase program.

The underlying credit metrics have remained relatively stable, which seems counterintuitive to the observed NAV and share price decline. There is no near-term maturity wall, with less than 1% of loans maturing this year and only 2.3% maturing next year, per the February monthly report:

But the February monthly report showed stabilizing signals: average loan spreads held at 3.16% vs. 3.19% in January, the CCC bucket declined from 4.5% to 4.2%, and the weighted average remaining reinvestment period actually increased from 3.3 to 3.4 years. Recurring cash distribution levels vs. the Q4 2025 figure of $0.61/share will be the key metric to watch in the Q1 2026 earnings release.

The current share price of $3.53 and February NAV midpoint of $4.36 imply a discount to NAV of ~19%. ECC has historically traded at an average premium of 9.86% since its 2014 IPO. The current discount is exceptionally wide by historical standards.

The shares have declined 60% from their 52-week high of $8.35 and have materially overshot the NAV decline in recent months. NAV fell 48% from Q4 2024 to February 2026. The share price fell ~60% over the same period as the market shifted from pricing ECC at a 6% premium to a 19% discount. A continued decline in the stock price without a corresponding NAV move would only widen this discount further.

The recently announced repurchase program is highly accretive: purchasing shares at $3.50 against a NAV of $4.36 creates $0.86 of instant NAV lift for remaining shareholders. CLOs have likely accumulated cash at the fund level, which can now be deployed at lower prices and significantly wider spreads. Recently reset or refinanced CLOs have between 3 and 5 years of reinvestment period remaining. CLO managers fee compensation is closely tied to the returns of the CLO equity tranche. Incentives are very much aligned on this, and Tier 1 managers typically aim to be in a position to deploy capital during periods of market dislocation.

CLO collateral managers deploy capital into the wider loan spread environment and purchase discounted loans in the primary and secondary markets. NAV recovers over the next 2 quarters as discounts narrow and distributions to CLO equity increase.

$5.50

$5.00–$5.50 (par to -10%)

Risks to Thesis

Continued NAV erosion if leveraged loan defaults accelerate, stressing OC cushions and CCC buckets. The current NAV discount and reduced distribution starting in Q2 2026 provide some cushion but do not fully protect against further NAV impairment.

CLO equity concentration in and of itself is a risk given the ~10x implied leverage of these residual cash flow tranches. ECC has actively diversified its holdings away from CLO equity in recent quarters but still has a 67% allocation.

AI disruption to software obligors (11.3% of underlying exposure) with seat-based SaaS and IT services business models is somewhat mitigated through the high level of diversification (1,850 obligors, 0.58% single name exposure).

Potential leverage and asset coverage level breach: The 1940 Act requires ECC to maintain asset coverage of at least 200% for preferred stock and 300% for debt securities. If coverage falls below these thresholds, ECC is prohibited from declaring common distributions and must redeem senior securities. The February 2026 NAV decline to $4.31–$4.41, reflecting approximately $176M in unrealized investment losses since Q4 2025 (132M shares × ($5.70 − $4.36), is a potential issue if left unaddressed. I expect ECC management will address this proactively by a partial conversion of the AA/AB convertible notes at NAV if needed.

The bear case that further NAV declines could trigger a forced deleveraging is structurally mitigated by the Series AA/AB convertible preferred. Management has the unilateral ability to convert ~$155M of senior securities to common equity, which improves asset coverage from ~192% to ~261% and restores significant leverage headroom.

Conclusion

The CFO/COO insider purchase of common shares comes at a critical time given the current market dislocations. It signals confidence that the book is properly marked and the panic driving the sell off is likely overdone. Furthermore, the mean reversion par build trade opportunity from private credit liquidations is actively improving the underlying portfolio quality, while the $100M buyback program is common shareholder friendly and deeply accretive at current price levels. For investors with a 12-month horizon and tolerance for mark-to-market volatility, the risk/reward is skewed to the upside.

Catalysts to monitor

March 2026 estimate for NAV and NII for Q1 2026, which is typically released by mid-April. Based on the March NAV performance of XFLT (down 2.8%), I expect ECC NAV to stabilize in March rather than a continuation of the significant decline ECC exhibited in February.

Buyback program execution pace and average purchase price, which can be estimated by the number of shares outstanding at the end of March. ECC common shares have traded at a more than a 15% discount to NAV in the second half of March, which should have triggered management to buy back shares but still needs to be confirmed.

Q1 2026 recurring cash distribution levels vs. Q4 2025 ($0.61/share) are expected to remain steady as loan spreads have stabilized and CLO liabilities were reset during the first quarter. This is a big unknown given the large drop in NAV during the month of February. The insider purchases provide some comfort that the underlying CLO equity positions were not experiencing a catastrophic, irreversible deterioration in credit quality.

data.title: No Shortage Of Volatility In Shortened Trading Week
data.description:
Originally published on April 02, 2026

Financial markets oscillate

Geopolitical developments in the Middle East continued to drive market performance. On Monday, rumblings that negotiations were taking place between the U.S. and Iran fuelled a significant rebound in equity markets. The S&P 500 Index climbed nearly 3% on the last trading day of Q1, while the Russell 2000 Index climbed nearly 3.5%.

Overall, the first quarter was a split-screen performance by global equity markets. In the first two months of the year, non-U.S. stocks outperformed U.S. stocks by roughly 10 percentage points. But in March, non-U.S. stocks underperformed by 6 percentage points as investors weighed the potential asymmetric impact of the war on various economies.

Uncertainty continues to remain elevated. On Wednesday evening, U.S. President Donald Trump gave a speech in which he indicated the war could wrap up shortly, but even so, it might persist for another 2-3 weeks. U.S. equity markets sold off overnight and into regular trading on Thursday morning, but market reaction flattened out towards the middle of the trading day amid reports that Iran and Oman might develop a joint monitoring protocol for ships in the Straits of Hormuz.

With equity markets being oversold but not yet panicked, we continue to believe that investors would be well-served by sticking close to their strategic asset allocations rather than making large tactical tilts.

Global manufacturing exhibits resilience

Despite the uncertainty caused by the war in the Middle East, the global manufacturing sector continues to exhibit strength. The Institute for Supply Management (ISM) Manufacturing PMI was 52.7% in March, edging higher from the February reading and coming in above consensus expectations. The New Orders subcomponent, often viewed as a leading indicator, was also in expansionary territory.

Outside of the U.S., the final Purchasing Managers’ Indices (PMI) data for March confirms some of the strength seen in the preliminary data released the previous weeks. Eurozone Manufacturing PMI published by S&P Global rose to 51.6 in March, the highest level since mid-2022. Meanwhile, manufacturing activity in the United Kingdom accelerated at a slower pace in March compared to February but still remained in net expansionary territory.

The resilience in global manufacturing is an encouraging silver lining during this period of heightened geopolitical risks.

U.S. labor markets show tentative signs of stabilization

U.S. labor markets are showing tentative signs of stabilization after the weak February payrolls report. Private sector data from Revelio Labs showed that the U.S. economy likely returned to net job creation in March, while job creation data from ADP came in above consensus expectations. In addition, private sector data from Gusto suggested that small businesses in the U.S. hired employees at the fastest pace since 2022.

Investors, of course, will also be paying close attention to the official March non-farm payrolls data that will be released on Friday. Should the payroll data match or exceed the consensus expectation for 60,000 jobs to be added, it would be yet another encouraging sign of the strength of the U.S. economy and reinforce that the Federal Reserve can afford to remain on an extended pause with interest rate movements.

U.S. markets will be closed when the payrolls data is released. This will also give investors more time to go beyond the headlines and dig into the details of the report. One key watchpoint: whether job creation can become more broad-based and expand beyond just the defensive sectors. The private sector data are leaning in this direction, but investors will need to see if the official payrolls numbers confirm this trend.

data.title: Blue Owl: Redemptions Panic Creates A Hard-To-Miss Opportunity (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital: Are we (finally) at peak redemptions?

I truly feel sorry for you, if you are a private credit investor, or if you invest in one of the alternative asset managers like Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL), with a relatively large direct lending portfolio.

As a reminder, direct lending fees accounted for >60% of the company's total adjusted revenue for 2025, definitely not an immaterial ratio by any measure.

Then when you add in the software forest fire into the mix, where investors believe that the industry is now ripe for disruption, it's really a case of a double whammy.

One that might just push the stock into a capitulation zone if we have another redemption event-related selloff that could test the conviction of the diamond hands still standing.

To which, I guess I do not have to remind you that I think we just had this particular redemption event that happened on Thursday, and it was reported all over the financial media.

Well, in case you missed it, you can refer to the shareholder letter that Blue Owl Credit Income Corp., or OCIC, issued to its investor class.

The under-fire asset manager has been facing a deluge of headwinds since the start of the year, which I also explained in my previous OWL write-up.

I rated the stock as a hold back then, as I wanted to assess whether we could get to what I thought was an optimal buy level with a massive washed-out bottom of sorts.

And based on what I’ve observed, I think we might just get one right now.

Back then, I indicated how Blue Owl Capital had been facing issues with OBDC II.

Private credit hit by massive quarterly redemptions

But, as we know, the widespread redemption requests aren’t just limited to OWL’s vehicles, but have also battered the whole industry.

For OCIC, the situation obviously couldn’t have been worse, but not unexpected, right?

While not specifically/directly mentioned, the recent reporting suggested that private wealth investors have been the driving force behind the exodus.

These investors have been bailing out from their private credit portfolio, and some weren’t even aware that they had private credit to begin with.

The panic has clearly percolated fear across the industry. Private wealth, who occupy 40% of Blue Owl Capital’s investor base, are arguably feeling anxious.

As seen with the redemption requests in the above chart, two of Blue Owl Capital’s funds have one of the largest shares of unmet redemptions compared to peers.

Notably, these unmet redemptions aren’t small, as OCIC "received an estimated repurchase request of 21.9% of shares outstanding during the first quarter."

In addition, OTIC got an "estimated 40.7% of total tender requests" in Q1.

Are we finally seeing the sum of all fears leading to the so-called peak redemption surge?

Anyway, I thought OWL management provided some much-needed assurances that the issues aren’t going to break the fund.

After all, for OTIC, the "$52M outflows represent less than 2% of its ~$3B net asset value."

Whereas for OCIC, the "$116M outflows represent less than 1% of the fund's NAV."

So, what’s the issue here?

Plus, management added more color, highlighting that it has substantive liquidity to meet future redemptions up to the 5% mark in subsequent quarters, which is in black and white, right?

Didn’t these more sophisticated private wealth investors understand that illiquidity isn’t a bug, but a feature?

Institutional investors, in my opinion, clearly understand this.

Which is why management even made the effort to underscore that within the investors who wanted out, it was a concentrated group, and relatively small in terms of percentage, that wanted to bail out:

Notably, this activity was driven by a relatively small minority of the investor base, with approximately 90% of our 90,000 shareholders electing not to tender and 1% of shareholders representing the majority of tenders. - OCIC April 2026 shareholder letter

Well, if you also have private credit exposure with your private banker, who may not have quite adequately explained to you the illiquidity feature (not bug), please don’t go freak out right now.

Anyway, even if you do try and run for the doors, I guess you’ve already seen how Blue Owl Capital management is now finally standing up to what these redemption limits are designed to do in the first place, which is to help control mass panic out of the door, right?

Blue Owl's Earnings Impact Likely Limited

And as Evercore explained in a recent commentary that I posted above, the earnings impact is "materially more modest," right?

So, while we may try to extrapolate on how OWL’s distributable earnings could be hurt from this private credit morass that it’s trying to navigate right now, I’m actually not too worried, as I think we could finally be at peak redemptions.

What do I mean? Meaning that the market now knows that even if they try and flood their redemption requests, they aren’t going to get them cleared anyway.

Asset managers know that they need to stand up to these actions and try to protect their business model from imploding.

Of course, it’s a double-edged sword; I get it.

For Blue Owl Capital, it’s also somewhat of a reckoning. Some of you may term it as the chicken finally came home to roost.

I thought that was kind of what Morningstar tried to do to characterize the situation, calling out possible hits to OWL’s credibility and also potentially hurting its future ability to raise funds.

I guess these aren’t unreasonable, right?

After all, it’s no secret that OWL’s 40% private wealth base will now start to think more carefully about allocating to Blue Owl’s private credit strategies.

And then, we have to consider that even Wall Street has downgraded OWL’s future earnings outlook, corroborating the less sanguine trajectory.

Plus, with 60% of its adjusted revenue emanating from direct lending fees, it’s only going to cause even more uncertainties in the forward outlook, isn't it? I'm sure you may point these contradictions out; I know.

Blue Owl: Still expected to post >12% earnings CAGR (2-year)

But, the market isn’t dumb, right?

With the earnings outlook already written down, OWL’s forward EPS outlook two years out looks to be slotting in on the lower side when compared to peers.

However, lower isn’t the same as negative growth, right? You know what I mean?

After all, we know that Blue Owl is already trying to further diversify and harden its resolve to build out the client base, deepening ties to family offices.

After all, family offices only accounted for 6% of its total asset base.

I think this represents a step up from just targeting the more "run-of-the-mill" private banking clientele that may be wealthy, but not as sophisticated as the ultra-high-net-worth, like those who own or operate family offices.

Also, consider that fundraising, while having slowed for the industry, hasn’t completely fallen to a trickle, right?

Even if the backdrop for the retail channel may be feeling somewhat more tipsy and angsty right now, I believe institutional investors aren’t all that worried.

Of course, we may have to consider the wide-ranging impact on the overall financial ecosystem and whether these effects could spread to the wider banking system.

But, based on the recent views from Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s most recent commentary, it doesn’t seem likely, right?

Plus, while some of the private credit investors want to flee, I guess the ability to gate these redemptions also demonstrates that the features are working out as intended.

This is to provide a more predictable stream of inflows and outflows that wouldn’t hobble the fund’s liquidity position, while still allowing them to deploy capital.

There’s no doubt that confidence in OWL’s strategies has been hit. Sure, you may even contend that Blue Owl Capital is going to have a much tougher time selling its strategies to private wealth from here. All reasonable and generally on point.

But, don’t you think that the market has already thought of these, as reflected in OWL’s valuations?

OWL: Valuations already hammered

Compared to peers with roughly similar market valuations, OWL trades at just <9.4x forward earnings.

And that’s lower than what we saw for TPG Inc. (TPG), which is valued at >13x forward earnings, and also The Carlyle Group (CG), valued at about 10.3x forward P/E.

It’s not an apples-to-apples comparison, but my point is that the market hasn’t been ignorant, right?

For a company that’s still expected to post >12% CAGR in earnings growth over the next two years, but is now rated below the financial sector median’s 10x forward earnings.

It appears to me that the market has simply overstated the earnings impact, based on these redemption requests and repurchases.

Plus, we have that capitulation point that I referred to, which I’m about to discuss next.

Buy Or Sell OWL Stock?

If you don't believe me, just take a glance at how quickly OWL investors fled the scene.

The last 5 months show price action that is reminiscent of huge selling pressure (read massive), one month after another.

It looks like just a vertical line down as the selling fervor went through the gutter, arguably.

And what matters is that we have a retest of that 2022 bottom, which also marks a complete turnaround.

And this reversal probably freaked out long-term (diamond hands) investors who held on to their shares even as the stock crumbled from the 2025 peak.

I mean, honestly, who would have thought such a hammering was possible?

But the truth is that in the market, anything is possible.

Especially when you're dealing with more opaque business models, such as in the private credit space.

Investors just do not know what they do not know.

They also don't know whether they can trust the NAV marks that the asset managers have assigned, as these are illiquid assets to begin with.

But, I also find it strange that the market finally felt the need to reflect the significant discount on this lack of liquidity feature, like as if it didn't matter previously.

Strange but true.

Nevertheless, while we can continue to ramble on about whether further fallout is possible, I urge you to keep your analysis and opportunities to the signal the market is giving you right now.

And the signal suggests that we have arguably reached maximum oversold levels.

With valuations already hammered into such attractive zones, if this is not the time to be greedy, I'm not sure how much longer you want to wait.

Plus, don't forget you get paid a forward yield of 10.3% to wait and ride the subsequent recovery. Not too bad, to be really honest.

Rating: Upgrade to Buy.

data.title: Q1 2026 Dividend Check-In: Highest Quarterly Hike Percentage Since 2019
data.description:
As Q1 2026 comes to a close, we follow up on an article we published last week on buybacks by analyzing corporations' other favorite way to return value to shareholders. The percentage of companies increasing dividends in Q1 was the highest level since Q1 2019 (45%). With 41% of dividend announcements denoting an increase, Q1 2026 tied with levels seen in Q1 2025 and Q1 2022. This suggests boardrooms are still optimistic about cash flows in 2026, as they enter the year with stronger balance sheets and positive earnings forecasts.

Global Dividend Changes: A High Hike-Percentage in Q1 2026

More dividend payouts mean investment teams need to adjust stock prices to reflect these corporate actions properly in order to conduct time-series analysis. TMX’s Price Adjustment Curve (PAC) provides price adjustments applicable down to tick-level prices or even orders, and below you can see the number of recorded adjustments for North American dividends have been steadily increasing. Q4 2025 recorded 17,229 such price adjustments, the largest quarterly tally in our five years of data. Q1 2026 ended with 13,137 price adjustments, the third largest amount.

A Dividend Divide: Large-Cap Confidence vs. Small-Cap Restraint?

Large Caps

While corporate confidence appears resilient among the world's largest giants, a deeper look at the data reveals a growing divide between the mega-cap leaders and smaller, more cautious firms. The start of 2026 has been marked by a show of strength from global heavyweights.

The most notable hikes this year have been from industry titans such as Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), AstraZeneca (AZN), HSBC Holdings (HSBC), and Verizon (VZ). For these companies, the message seems to be, "We have more cash than we know what to do with, and we are confident in the year ahead." Companies with market capitalizations over $10B (large cap) and $200B (mega cap) are leading the charge, with over 60% of them reporting increases. This indicates that industry leaders are prioritizing shareholder returns and possess the cash flow stability to do so.

Small Caps

While the giants are hiking, the rest of the market is being notably more conservative. Small-cap companies (under $2B) only managed a 38% increase rate, with a high percentage of "No Change" announcements (43%). This suggests that smaller firms are bracing for tighter credit conditions or slower growth and are choosing to hoard cash rather than reward shareholders.

Selective Cuts: High-Profile Retreats and Regional Shifts

Perhaps the most interesting part of the story is the 11% of companies that cut their dividends. Even among the large caps, we saw some surprising retreats. High-profile names like China Mobile, ING Groep and Barclays recorded decreases in this period.

Regionally, countries like New Zealand, Türkiye, Israel, Australia and Brazil saw a higher frequency of dividend cuts vs. dividend increases, suggesting that cash preservation is top of mind in other parts of the world.

The Bottom Line

Dividend-increase announcements are tracking above year-ago levels and 2024’s pace. Even with shakiness in the labor market and the uncertainty caused by the Iran war, shareholders are being rewarded with growing quarterly payouts.

We’ll find out more in the weeks ahead—first from this week’s employment numbers and second as first-quarter earnings reports roll in (assuming we are sticking with quarterly updates!).

data.title: Kuaishou Technology: Downgrade To Hold As Near-Term Setup Is Poor
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Kuaishou Technology (KUASF) was a buy rating because revenue growth remains robust across key segments, even in live streaming. The AI growth driver was also becoming meaningful. However, as much as the Q4 quarter itself was solid, the near-term setup has clearly worsened. Management’s 2026 guidance now points to slower growth, lower margins, and a meaningful decline in adj. earnings as AI capex rises sharply. I still think the long-term story is intact, but the near-term setup is poor here.

4Q25 earnings review

The quarter itself was solid. Total revenue grew 12% y/y, with all segments contributing. In online marketing services [OMS], revenue grew 15% y/y, while other services, which are mainly e-commerce-related, grew 28% y/y. For e-commerce, GMV grew 13% y/y. Unlike Q3, live streaming was the weak spot, with revenue down 2% y/y. Nonetheless, topline growth did flow through to profits. Gross profit grew almost in line with revenue growth, at 14% y/y, but on an adjusted basis, net income was up 16% y/y to RMB5.46 billion.

The quarter was fine, but the near-term setup isn’t great

The reason why I downgrade KUASF to hold is because the near-term setup has gotten worse. Management guided 2026 revenue growth of 4% to 4.5%. Within that, OMS is expected to grow just 6% to 6.5%, live broadcasting to decline 6.5%, and other services to grow 15%, with e-commerce up in the high single digits. Adj. earnings is guided to RMB17 billion to RMB17.5 billion, which implies an 11% to 11.5% net margin and a 15% to 18% y/y decline. Importantly, capex is expected to swell from RMB14.9 billion in 2025 to RMB26 billion in 2026.

This changes the equity narrative in a huge way. Back in my Q3 update, the narrative was bullish because revenue growth was improving, live streaming had stabilized, and AI was mostly being framed as a monetization and margin support story. While I did flag higher AI capex, Q4 slowdown, and Kling competition, they were not the focus since growth was accelerating. However, the latter points are now becoming the main focus, in my view. The market is now focusing on whether the earnings pressure tied to this AI investment phase will last even longer.

That is why I am positioning myself to be neutral in the near term. The core business still looks healthy, but the near-term earnings math has clearly worsened. That said, I also would not turn bearish because nothing in the quarter suggested the business model was breaking structurally. The issue is not that KUASF suddenly stopped growing. The issue, I believe, is that the cost of chasing the next leg of growth is now being "quantified," and that appears to be much more than what the market is expecting (as you can see from the earnings downwards revision chart in my valuation section below).

Kling and capital returns

Now, for investors that are positioning themselves to be bearish here, I urge against that. The strongest reason not to become negative is Kling. Kling is still a very fast-growing business within KUASF, growing >10% sequentially in Q4 2025.

In the April summit (credits to Morgan Stanley), Kling’s January 2026 ARR had already reached $300 million, and management believes guidance for Kling’s FY2026 revenue to double is conservative. From this, we can safely infer that the investments made to KUASF’s AI strategy are generating returns, and it is not as if management is burning capital. It does suggest that revenue can scale fast enough, as FY2026 revenue (for Kling) appears to be able to grow >2x. Competition here is also less severe than I thought. I was particularly worried about Sora 2, ByteDance, and Baidu, but management explicitly noted that said price competition has already eased, peers are reducing discounts, and Kling sustained sequential ARR growth even after Seedance 2.0 launched. All of this should weaken the extreme bear case for Kling, which is that this becomes a pure price war with little economic value.

In addition, KUASF is still returning cash even as AI spending rises sharply. Despite the capex increase, management announced a HK$0.69 final dividend, together with an interim special dividend and a HK$3.1 billion buyback, and it still targets overall positive free cash flow in 2026. These actions basically serve as a floor to valuation and, in my opinion, make shorting the stock here hard. There is also the risk that management could invest less than expected if the results are already good enough. In that scenario, KUASF could easily beat consensus earnings estimates and return even more cash to shareholders.

Valuation

The narrative has now shifted from “focus on the growth potential” to “the cost of growth over the near term," I believe, and that weighs on the stock’s valuation. I do believe that if we look over the medium term, the current ~9x forward PE may look cheap. But if we just look at FY2026, earnings are going to see a huge decline before the growth. Look at the consensus estimates below; they have been revised downwards sharply, and I believe this will be the market’s focus for the near term. Current FY2026 adj. EPS estimates call for RMB4.25, which, if you attach a 9x forward PE to it, is about ~RMB38, or ~$5.60. Given this dynamic, I am now shifting to neutral for the near-term.

Conclusion

I am downgrading KUASF to a hold rating. I do not think the business is broken. OMS and e-commerce-related revenue are still growing well; Kling is scaling quickly, and capital returns should make it harder to turn outright bearish. But I also believe the market is now focused on how much earnings pressure this AI investment phase will create over the near term. Until that pressure starts to ease, I think a neutral view makes the most sense.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CMB.TECH: Valuation Implies Mid-Cycle - Reality Looks Stronger
data.description:
Introduction

A quick look at CMB.TECH’s (CMBT) recent earnings data may confuse investors because, on the one hand, revenue has gone up and EBITDA is strong, while on the other hand, EPS has fallen sharply.

CMBT may appear under pressure if you only go by that data, but that will be a wrong assessment.

It will be wrong because the weak earnings largely came from one-off costs related to the Golden Ocean deal. That involved refinancing and integration expenses, but more importantly, only focusing on EPS misses the point that CMBT is no longer a business whose earnings are determined by accounting but by freight rates.

Once we get that shift, the apparent contradiction goes away. CMBT’s earnings power is actually much higher than the reported EPS suggests. But it is also more sensitive to certain market conditions.

Why Reported EPS Is Misleading

CMBT’s full-year net income was around $140 million. Their revenue was around $1.67 billion, and EBITDA was $943 million. This shows a disconnect between revenue and income. The latter declined over 80% YoY, while EPS declined over 84%.

However, this decline is not what we see in the underlying business.

In their earnings call, management clearly identifies a few non-recurring items that caused this decline. These include items listed in the section below, and they should not be used to extrapolate future earnings. These distort the correct view of current profitability. CMBT is in a rapid restructuring phase, so these are not reflective of its steady-state earnings capacity.

Rebuilding Normalized Earnings

We need to adjust for these one-offs in order to get an idea of underlying performance. Based on the company’s February 2026 6-K disclosures:

Some items were not explicitly disclosed, so I would round that to $50 million, so we get an adjusted figure of roughly:

$140M + ~$50M ≈ ~$190M

This is a more reasonable estimate of normalized earnings for the period. However, even this is conservative, because this is from a transition year - in fact, the quarter where management says most of the one-off expenses were concentrated. 2025 saw them do portfolio changes, fleet changes, and a major transaction. That means the number $190 million above removes some of the visible distortions, but it doesn’t yet capture the full earnings potential.

Moving Beyond EPS: EBITDA as a Better Anchor

A better way would be to focus on operating performance.

The revenue/EBITDA/income numbers I mentioned earlier included the following items:

That means, while reported net income was around $140 million, the adjusted figure is closer to $220 million.

This is my baseline. Since CMBT is largely exposed to spot markets, small increases in freight rates flow disproportionately to the bottom line. For example, at a mid-to-high cycle TCE level, it could move towards $300–350 million.

However, even an EBITDA-based framework does not tell the full story. The problem is that it still treats earnings as a stable quantity - which it is not. CMBT’s earnings are highly variable and depend on market conditions.

CMBT’s past financials clearly show it was always highly exposed to spot rates. After the merger, the scale of that exposure has increased sharply. So, instead of asking — “what are the earnings” — we need to ask — “What are the earnings at a given level of freight rates?” So we will develop a rate-based model next.

Larger Scale and Higher Rate Exposure

Even before the merger, CMBT was largely spot business. In 2024, their spot exposure was roughly 75% of 18,500 shipping days, as inferred from the source referenced above. In 2026, after the merger, that number nearly tripled. Now they expect 53,000 shipping days, 83% of which – or 44,000 days – are spot business.

This is not a shift from contracted to spot, but from a high spot exposure to very high. But more fundamentally, this is combined with a near tripling of operating scale.

With such a large scale of spot exposure, revenue is highly sensitive to market conditions. Any change in freight rates goes directly towards realized earnings.

This tells us why traditional metrics like trailing EPS can give us the wrong picture. They give us a snapshot of an earlier and smaller company – that too, distorted by one-offs. More material to understanding today’s post-merger CMBT is to discuss the rate environment into which those 44,000 spot-exposed days will be deployed.

CMBT is now more earnings sensitive than ever before, and this is how we must model it.

The core model: freight rates and earnings

CMBT’s core business, removed from accounting noise, is simple. Everything follows from time charter equivalent (TCE) rates. This is applied across a very large base of spot-exposed shipping days. Of course, if we are to be precise, the entire formula is:

Earnings = (Spot TCE × Spot days) + (Fixed rates × Contract days) – costs

On the cost side, operating costs were around $10,790 per day (2024 figures). While we do not have precise figures for 2025 (shipping days changed post-merger), this figure is likely going to remain more or less the same, ignoring some efficiencies of scale. This aligns with CMBT’s $400+ million reported cost base. Once their earnings cross that base, any additional revenue goes directly to profit. This creates a significant operating leverage.

Using this framework, we can construct a simple sensitivity model:

The table shows that net income more than doubles when rates increase 70% from $35,000/day to $60,000/day.

So our key takeaway here is that CMBT’s earnings are highly sensitive to freight rates — and that sensitivity is not linear. Once the cost base gets covered, everything over and above goes towards profits. But the opposite is also true. When rates go down, costs still remain more or less fixed. They do not go down with rates as much. So margins compress quickly. This makes CMBT a high risk-reward business, not a steady compounder.

Management guidance also conforms to this rate-driven framework. Management said that under current conditions they could generate ~$700 million in free cash flow. This coincides with the base-to-bull case here, where higher freight rates will help generate several hundred million dollars of net income and cash flow.

Valuation and freight rates

CMBT’s earnings are closely aligned with freight rates. That means traditional valuation metrics must also be tied to freight rates to give a proper picture. So, a forward P/E of 4–9x doesn’t mean much unless we specify a particular TCE that the market is discounting.

Taking CMBT’s current market cap at ~$3.6 billion, we can arrive at implied earnings using different scenarios. We can take a fixed 6x multiple because it is reasonable for mid-cycle and is within the range of current forward multiples. Using that, we get a normalized net income of :

$3.6B ÷ 6 = ~$600M

Even if we use a more conservative 8x multiple, implied earnings are still ~$450M. Using the earlier sensitivity framework:

The table above shows that these rates lie somewhere between a mid-cycle to a slightly elevated freight rate – not extreme conditions.

This positioning is important:

The stock depends heavily on where freight rates go next. Management has guided towards $700 million free cash flow, which is a little above the implied earnings range here of $450–600 million. That implies, either current conditions are stronger than what the valuation assumes, or the market is discounting cyclicality.

Freight Markets: The Only Variable That Matters

There are two distinct stories here. On the tanker side, rates are being driven by geopolitics, not normal seasonality. Severe disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, earlier disruptions in the Red Sea, etc., have all tightened tanker availability. Tankers from the Atlantic Basin are now plying to Asia and Europe. According to various sources, Suezmax rates were up to around $166k–170k/day in early March. These rates may be temporary, but while they last, CMBT has very high earnings capacity through its tanker fleet.

Dry bulk doesn’t move up as much but is more durable. Management in the latest earnings call has said that in 2026, dry bulk business will come from longer-haul iron ore and bauxite, mainly from West Africa. Low orderbook-to-fleet ratios and aging fleets also indicate reduced supply and, therefore, higher freight rates over time. Their past two years’ earnings calls show strong support from Atlantic iron ore and Guinea bauxite flows. The Baltic dry market is softer and more volatile than tankers, but it is still supply-constrained, as fundamentals from Capesizes and Newcastlemaxes show. In other words, tankers give spikes, but dry bulk offers cycle support.

This is an important distinction because CMBT is neither a pure-play tanker nor a pure-play bulk operator. So each setup acts as a hedge for the other, and while dry bulk gives it anchor, tankers give it momentum.

Given the situation in Hormuz, which may turn into a protracted affair, investors must watch out for how soon disruption goes away or whether this becomes a longer routing regime. The other forward-looking item to watch is how long the Atlantic basin crude export stays elevated, keeping tanker ton-miles high. A third item is China-linked iron ore and Guinea bauxite flow. This keeps dry bulk moving, and this is important because if dry bulk weakens and tanker stress normalizes at the same time, earnings will compress. If dry bulk holds steady while tanker uptick stays longer, CMBT can hold onto a rate regime stronger than trough assumptions.

Risks

When rate conditions change, the same structure that creates upside can then create downside. Since earnings are heavily tied to freight rates, the primary risk is when rates fall. A move from ~$40–50k/day toward ~$20k/day will compress net income severely. If it happens, it won’t be a gradual change. It will be a sharp fall because costs are largely fixed, so decline in revenue will disproportionately hit the bottom line.

Beyond rates, the broader shipping cycle is highly volatile. Global trade, commodity demand (especially from dry bulk), fleet supply – and in today’s world, geopolitical disruptions – drive freight markets. Some level of disruption may favor the industry; however, if these normalize, there could be a sudden decline.

The company is actively spending capex on new ventures like offshore and ammonia to maintain dividend flexibility. However, these introduce execution risks.

Also, current valuation implies a mid-to-high cycle environment (~$35k–45k/day). That leaves little margin of error if rates fall. Operational and balance sheet strengths do matter, but in this case, they are secondary to freight rates - something not always in CMBT’s control. This makes CMBT a high-conviction but volatile investment where upside is directly linked to where the shipping cycle is being driven by macro factors.

Conclusion

CMBT’s reported earnings do not properly reflect its earnings power, which is directly tied to freight rates and over 80% spot exposure. The setup is favorable given current energy flow disruptions. Freight rates will stay elevated for the foreseeable future, and will drive earnings and cash flow well above the current baseline. In such a scenario, CMBT is a compelling investment to capture the current strength in shipping markets, and should remain so over the next 12-18 months, while the turmoil in the Middle East continues to disrupt energy flow.
